name: Birch Beginning
description: The Birch, revered as 'Beith' in the Ogham alphabet, marks the beginning of the Celtic year and symbolizes new beginnings, purification, and renewal. Its slender white trunk reaching skyward connects the earthly and spiritual realms. In Celtic lore, the birch is associated with the Spring Equinox (Alban Eilir), representing the return of light and warmth after the dark winter months. It is believed to possess protective qualities, warding off negative energies and attracting good fortune. Birch branches were traditionally used in rituals to cleanse spaces and individuals, sweeping away old energies to make way for the new. Cradleboards were often made of birch wood, symbolizing the protection and nurturing of new life. The tree's resilience and ability to thrive in harsh conditions further contribute to its symbolism of strength and adaptability. Birch sap was valued for its medicinal properties and was believed to purify the blood. The bark, being naturally waterproof, was used for writing and crafting, carrying messages and preserving knowledge. The Birch Beginning represents a potent symbol of hope, resilience, and the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth within the Celtic worldview. It embodies the spirit of growth, potential, and the enduring power of nature. Its association with feminine energy and fertility makes it a sacred tree deeply connected to the Earth Mother and the nurturing aspects of creation.
subtitle: New beginnings, purification, and renewal.

keyMyths.title: The Birch and the Spring Equinox
keyMyths.description: The Birch is the first tree in the Ogham alphabet and represents the start of the Celtic year, directly coinciding with the Spring Equinox, heralding the return of life and light. It symbolizes the triumph of good over evil and the promise of new beginnings after winter's darkness.

sources.author: Miranda Green
sources.work: Myths and Symbols in Pagan Europe
sources.description: Discusses the significance of trees in Celtic symbolism and ritual.
sources.text: Discusses the significance of trees in Celtic symbolism and ritual.

sources.author: Philip Carr-Gomm
